div.h2: Why GPTQ Uses Diagonal for Practical Quantization

div.p: For a Linear layer Y = XWᵀ, the Hessian of the output error:

ul.li: X[j,i] = activation at position j, feature i
H_diag[i] = sensitivity of weight column i This is the Fisher Information Matrix diagonal for squared error loss.

div.p: Key Insight: The diagonal approximation is just for the INITIAL inverse. GPTQ uses Cholesky decomposition of the full Hessian for accurate compensation updates, not just the diagonal.

text.tspan: # Hessian diagonal: sum of squared inputs per column
# - High H_diag[i] → weight i affects output strongly
# - Low H_diag[i] → weight i has minimal impact
# Hessian diagonal: sum of squared inputs per column
# - High H_diag[i] → weight i affects output strongly
# - Low H_diag[i] → weight i has minimal impact
# Knows HOW MUCH each weight matters
# Knows HOW MUCH each weight matters
# Does NOT know how weights correlate
# "If we change weight i, how much
# Does NOT know how weights correlate
# "If we change weight i, how much
# This is where interaction is recovered!

g.text: W[:, i+1:] -= error @ H_inv[i, i+1:] / H_inv[i, i]

text.tspan: # The Cholesky factor H_inv encodes interactions
# This is where interaction is recovered!
# The Cholesky factor H_inv encodes interactions
